talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|14th shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate of Japan}} {{family name hatnote|[[Tokugawa (surname)|Tokugawa]]|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Officeholder | honorific_prefix = [[Senior First Rank]] | name = Tokugawa Iemochi | native_name = {{Nobold|徳川 家茂}} | native_name_lang = ja | image = Toku14-2.jpg | order = ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' | monarch = [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Kōmei]] | term_start = 14 August 1858 | term_end = 29 August 1866 | predecessor = [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] | successor = [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | spouse = [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]] | birth_date = {{Birth date|1846|7|17|df=y}} | birth_place = [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato]], [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Japan | death_date = {{death date and age|1866|8|29|1846|7|17|df=y}} | death_place = [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]], Japan | signature = Tokugawa Iemochi kao.jpg }} {{nihongo|'''Tokugawa Iemochi'''|{{linktext|徳川}} {{linktext|家茂}}}} (17 July 1846 – 29 August 1866) was the 14th ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], who held office from 1858 to 1866. {{cite web |url=https://www.pbs.org/empires/japan/timeline_1800.html |title=Japan:Memoirs of a Secret Empire |access-date=2007-08-11 |work= PBS}} During his reign there was much internal turmoil as a result of the "re-opening" of Japan to western nations. Iemochi's reign also saw a weakening of the shogunate. Iemochi died in 1866 and was buried in [[:en:Zōjō-ji|Zōjō-ji]]. His Buddhist name was Shonmyoin. ==Biography== Iemochi, known in his childhood as Kikuchiyo (菊千代), was the eldest son of the 11th-generation [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] lord [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) with his concubine known as Jitsujoin and was born in the domain's residence in Edo (modern-day [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato-ku]] in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]). Nariyuki was a younger son of the 11th ''shōgun'', [[:en:Tokugawa Ienari|Tokugawa Ienari]]. In 1847, at age 1, he was adopted as the heir of the 12th-generation ''daimyō'' [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]], and succeeded him in 1850, taking the name Tokugawa Yoshitomi following his coming of age in 1851. In 1858 he had audience with ''shōgun'' Iesada and his wife, Atsuhime short after he was adopted as their son and named as the successor to the main Tokugawa house. The choice of Yoshitomi was not without conflict; there were other factions in the government who supported [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] or [[:en:Matsudaira Naritami|Matsudaira Naritami]] for ''shōgun''; both of them, unlike Yoshitomi, were adults. After assuming the office of shogun, Yoshitomi changed his name to Iemochi. Before Iesada died he give his will to [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]], that: * First, [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] must help Iemochi at administration until Iemochi was old enough to rule. * Second, all political issues must be discussed with [[:en:Tenshoin|Tenshoin]], as Iemochi's mother. As part of the ''[[:en:Kōbu gattai|Kōbu gattai]]'' ("Union of Court and Bakufu") movement, On 11 February 1862, Iemochi married [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]], daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ninko|Emperor Ninko]]. Princess Kazu refused to use the title "[[:en:Midaidokoro|Midaidokoro]]", and instead only used the title "Miya". On 22 April 1863 (''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]] 3, 5th day of the 3rd month''), ''shōgun'' Iemochi traveled in a great procession to the capital. He had been summoned by the emperor, and had 3,000 retainers as escort. This was the first time since the visit of [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]] in the ''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]]'' era, 230 years before, that a shogun had visited Kyoto.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: the Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 325. His early death at the age of 20 put an end to his short marriage with princess Kazu-no-Miya. Before he died he adopted a son, Tayasu Kamenosuke (later known as [[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]]), as his heir. At that time Tayasu Kamenosuke was only 3 years old, but as the Tokugawa shogunate was [[:en:Second Chōshū expedition|at war with Chōshū]], the adult [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] was appointed the fifteenth ''shōgun''. ''Shōgun'' Yoshinobu then adopted Iemochi's adopted son, Tayasu Kamenosuke. After Iemochi's death, Kazu-no-Miya changed her name to Seikan'in no Miya. The cause of Iemochi's death is widely reported as heart failure due to [[:en:Beriberi|Beriberi]], a disease caused by [[:en:thiamine|thiamine]] (vitamin B1) deficiency. His successor, Tokugawa Yoshinobu, was the last Tokugawa ''shōgun'' and witnessed the end of the shogunate, which gave way to the [[Meiji Restoration]]. ==Family== * Father: [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) * Mother: [[Jitsujoin]] (1821–1904) * Adoptive fathers: ** [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] (1820–1849) ** [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] * Adoptive Mother: [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Wife: [[:en:Kazu-no-Miya Chikako|Kazu-no-Miya Chikako]] * Concubine: ** [[Oyuri no Kata]] (1850–1880) later Shoko-in ** Ohina no Kata (1846–1862) daughter of Shimazu Tadafuyu also niece of [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Adopted son: **[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] **[[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]] ==Eras of Iemochi's ''bakufu''== [[File:Abraham Lincoln to the Shogun of Japan 14 November 1861.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Abraham Lincoln|Abraham Lincoln]] to ''shōgun'' Tokugawa Iemochi announcing the departure of [[:en:Townsend Harris|Townsend Harris]]. 14 November 1861.]] [[File:Letter of Napoleon III to the Japanese Shogun to introduce Leon Roches in replacement of Duchesne de Bellecourt.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Napoleon III|Napoleon III]] to "Taïcoun" Tokugawa Iemochi nominating [[:en:Léon Roches|Léon Roches]], in replacement of [[:en:Duchesne de Bellecourt|Duchesne de Bellecourt]], 27 October 1863. [[:en:Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)]].]] The years in which Iemochi was ''shōgun'' are more specifically identified by more than one [[:en:Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[:en:Nengō|Nengō]]''. * ''[[:en:Ansei|Ansei]]'' (1854–1860) * ''[[:en:Man'en|Man'en]]'' (1860–1861) * ''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]]'' (1861–1864) * ''[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]]'' (1864–1865) * ''[[:en:Keiō|Keiō]]'' (1865–1868) ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== *''Rekishi Dokuhon'' Jan. 2006 issue: ''Tokugawa Shōgun-ke to Matsudaira Ichizoku'' *''Tokugawa Iemochi to sono jidai: wakaki shōgun no shōgai'' 徳川家茂とその時代: 若き将軍の生涯. Tokyo: Tokugawa kin'en zaidan 徳川記念財団, 2007. * Totman, Conrad. (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tMFPbDrEPtkC ''The Collapse of the Tokugawa Bakufu, 1862–1868.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawai'i Press|University of Hawai'i Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-0614-9}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline}} * Digitized woodblock print: [http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm Shogun Iemochi in procession towards Kyoto in ''Bunkyū 3''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071206043308/http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm |date=2007-12-06 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy}} {{succession box | title=[[:en:Kishū Domain|Lord of Kishū]]:
Tokugawa Iemochi | before=[[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] | after=[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-mil}} {{succession box | title=''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'':
Tokugawa Iemochi | before=[[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] | after=[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-end}} {{Tokugawa dynasty (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa chronology (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa_officials}} {{Shoguns}} {{Authority control}} ‎]] {{draft categories| }} [[en:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ar:توكوغاوا إييموتشي]] [[ca:Tokugawa Iemochi]] {{Commons category|Tokugawa Iemochi}} [[cs:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[da:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[de:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[es:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[eu:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fa:توکوگاوا ایه‌موچی]] [[fi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fo:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[hu:Tokugava Iemocsi]] [[id:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[it:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ja:徳川家茂]] [[ko:도쿠가와 이에모치]] [[no:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[pl:Iemochi Tokugawa]] [[pt:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ru:Токугава Иэмоти]] [[sk:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[sr:Токугава Ијемочи]] [[sv:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[th:โทกูงาวะ อิเอโมจิ]] [[tr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[uk:Токуґава Іємоті]] [[vi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[wuu:德川家茂]] [[zh:德川家茂]] [[zh-min-nan:Tokugawa Iemoti]] [[zh-yue:德川家茂]] {{wikidata link|Q348466}} [[Category:1846 births]] [[Category:1866 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century shōguns]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:People from Minato, Tokyo]] [[Category:People of Bakumatsu]] [[Category:Tokugawa clan]] [[Category:Tokugawa shōguns]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|14th shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate of Japan}} {{family name hatnote|[[Tokugawa (surname)|Tokugawa]]|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Officeholder | honorific_prefix = [[Senior First Rank]] | name = Tokugawa Iemochi | native_name = {{Nobold|徳川 家茂}} | native_name_lang = ja | image = Toku14-2.jpg | order = ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' | monarch = [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Kōmei]] | term_start = 14 August 1858 | term_end = 29 August 1866 | predecessor = [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] | successor = [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | spouse = [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]] | birth_date = {{Birth date|1846|7|17|df=y}} | birth_place = [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato]], [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Japan | death_date = {{death date and age|1866|8|29|1846|7|17|df=y}} | death_place = [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]], Japan | signature = Tokugawa Iemochi kao.jpg }} {{nihongo|'''Tokugawa Iemochi'''|{{linktext|徳川}} {{linktext|家茂}}}} (17 July 1846 – 29 August 1866) was the 14th ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], who held office from 1858 to 1866. {{cite web |url=https://www.pbs.org/empires/japan/timeline_1800.html |title=Japan:Memoirs of a Secret Empire |access-date=2007-08-11 |work= PBS}} During his reign there was much internal turmoil as a result of the "re-opening" of Japan to western nations. Iemochi's reign also saw a weakening of the shogunate. Iemochi died in 1866 and was buried in [[:en:Zōjō-ji|Zōjō-ji]]. His Buddhist name was Shonmyoin. ==Biography== Iemochi, known in his childhood as Kikuchiyo (菊千代), was the eldest son of the 11th-generation [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] lord [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) with his concubine known as Jitsujoin and was born in the domain's residence in Edo (modern-day [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato-ku]] in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]). Nariyuki was a younger son of the 11th ''shōgun'', [[:en:Tokugawa Ienari|Tokugawa Ienari]]. In 1847, at age 1, he was adopted as the heir of the 12th-generation ''daimyō'' [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]], and succeeded him in 1850, taking the name Tokugawa Yoshitomi following his coming of age in 1851. In 1858 he had audience with ''shōgun'' Iesada and his wife, Atsuhime short after he was adopted as their son and named as the successor to the main Tokugawa house. The choice of Yoshitomi was not without conflict; there were other factions in the government who supported [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] or [[:en:Matsudaira Naritami|Matsudaira Naritami]] for ''shōgun''; both of them, unlike Yoshitomi, were adults. After assuming the office of shogun, Yoshitomi changed his name to Iemochi. Before Iesada died he give his will to [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]], that: * First, [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] must help Iemochi at administration until Iemochi was old enough to rule. * Second, all political issues must be discussed with [[:en:Tenshoin|Tenshoin]], as Iemochi's mother. As part of the ''[[:en:Kōbu gattai|Kōbu gattai]]'' ("Union of Court and Bakufu") movement, On 11 February 1862, Iemochi married [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]], daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ninko|Emperor Ninko]]. Princess Kazu refused to use the title "[[:en:Midaidokoro|Midaidokoro]]", and instead only used the title "Miya". On 22 April 1863 (''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]] 3, 5th day of the 3rd month''), ''shōgun'' Iemochi traveled in a great procession to the capital. He had been summoned by the emperor, and had 3,000 retainers as escort. This was the first time since the visit of [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]] in the ''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]]'' era, 230 years before, that a shogun had visited Kyoto.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: the Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 325. His early death at the age of 20 put an end to his short marriage with princess Kazu-no-Miya. Before he died he adopted a son, Tayasu Kamenosuke (later known as [[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]]), as his heir. At that time Tayasu Kamenosuke was only 3 years old, but as the Tokugawa shogunate was [[:en:Second Chōshū expedition|at war with Chōshū]], the adult [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] was appointed the fifteenth ''shōgun''. ''Shōgun'' Yoshinobu then adopted Iemochi's adopted son, Tayasu Kamenosuke. After Iemochi's death, Kazu-no-Miya changed her name to Seikan'in no Miya. The cause of Iemochi's death is widely reported as heart failure due to [[:en:Beriberi|Beriberi]], a disease caused by [[:en:thiamine|thiamine]] (vitamin B1) deficiency. His successor, Tokugawa Yoshinobu, was the last Tokugawa ''shōgun'' and witnessed the end of the shogunate, which gave way to the [[Meiji Restoration]]. ==Family== * Father: [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) * Mother: [[Jitsujoin]] (1821–1904) * Adoptive fathers: ** [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] (1820–1849) ** [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] * Adoptive Mother: [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Wife: [[:en:Kazu-no-Miya Chikako|Kazu-no-Miya Chikako]] * Concubine: ** [[Oyuri no Kata]] (1850–1880) later Shoko-in ** Ohina no Kata (1846–1862) daughter of Shimazu Tadafuyu also niece of [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Adopted son: **[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] **[[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]] ==Eras of Iemochi's ''bakufu''== [[File:Abraham Lincoln to the Shogun of Japan 14 November 1861.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Abraham Lincoln|Abraham Lincoln]] to ''shōgun'' Tokugawa Iemochi announcing the departure of [[:en:Townsend Harris|Townsend Harris]]. 14 November 1861.]] [[File:Letter of Napoleon III to the Japanese Shogun to introduce Leon Roches in replacement of Duchesne de Bellecourt.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Napoleon III|Napoleon III]] to "Taïcoun" Tokugawa Iemochi nominating [[:en:Léon Roches|Léon Roches]], in replacement of [[:en:Duchesne de Bellecourt|Duchesne de Bellecourt]], 27 October 1863. [[:en:Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)]].]] The years in which Iemochi was ''shōgun'' are more specifically identified by more than one [[:en:Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[:en:Nengō|Nengō]]''. * ''[[:en:Ansei|Ansei]]'' (1854–1860) * ''[[:en:Man'en|Man'en]]'' (1860–1861) * ''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]]'' (1861–1864) * ''[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]]'' (1864–1865) * ''[[:en:Keiō|Keiō]]'' (1865–1868) ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== *''Rekishi Dokuhon'' Jan. 2006 issue: ''Tokugawa Shōgun-ke to Matsudaira Ichizoku'' *''Tokugawa Iemochi to sono jidai: wakaki shōgun no shōgai'' 徳川家茂とその時代: 若き将軍の生涯. Tokyo: Tokugawa kin'en zaidan 徳川記念財団, 2007. * Totman, Conrad. (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tMFPbDrEPtkC ''The Collapse of the Tokugawa Bakufu, 1862–1868.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawai'i Press|University of Hawai'i Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-0614-9}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline}} * Digitized woodblock print: [http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm Shogun Iemochi in procession towards Kyoto in ''Bunkyū 3''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071206043308/http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm |date=2007-12-06 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy}} {{succession box | title=[[:en:Kishū Domain|Lord of Kishū]]:
Tokugawa Iemochi | before=[[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] | after=[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-mil}} {{succession box | title=''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'':
Tokugawa Iemochi | before=[[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] | after=[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-end}} {{Tokugawa dynasty (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa chronology (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa_officials}} {{Shoguns}} {{Authority control}} ‎]] [[en:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ar:توكوغاوا إييموتشي]] [[ca:Tokugawa Iemochi]] {{Commons category|Tokugawa Iemochi}} [[cs:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[da:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[de:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[es:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[eu:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fa:توکوگاوا ایه‌موچی]] [[fi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fo:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[hu:Tokugava Iemocsi]] [[id:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[it:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ja:徳川家茂]] [[ko:도쿠가와 이에모치]] [[no:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[pl:Iemochi Tokugawa]] [[pt:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ru:Токугава Иэмоти]] [[sk:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[sr:Токугава Ијемочи]] [[sv:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[th:โทกูงาวะ อิเอโมจิ]] [[tr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[uk:Токуґава Іємоті]] [[vi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[wuu:德川家茂]] [[zh:德川家茂]] [[zh-min-nan:Tokugawa Iemoti]] [[zh-yue:德川家茂]] {{wikidata link|Q348466}} [[Category:1846 births]] [[Category:1866 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century shōguns]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:People from Minato, Tokyo]] [[Category:People of Bakumatsu]] [[Category:Tokugawa clan]] [[Category:Tokugawa shōguns]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

July 1846 – 29 August 1866) was the 14th ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], who held office from 1858 to 1866. {{cite web |url=https://www.pbs.org/empires/japan/timeline_1800.html |title=Japan:Memoirs of a Secret Empire |access-date=2007-08-11 |work= PBS}} During his reign there was much internal turmoil as a result of the "re-opening" of Japan to western nations. Iemochi's reign also saw a weakening of the shogunate. Iemochi died in 1866 and was buried in [[:en:Zōjō-ji|Zōjō-ji]]. His Buddhist name was Shonmyoin. ==Biography== Iemochi, known in his childhood as Kikuchiyo (菊千代), was the eldest son of the 11th-generation [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] lord [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) with his concubine known as Jitsujoin and was born in the domain's residence in Edo (modern-day [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato-ku]] in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]). Nariyuki was a younger son of the 11th ''shōgun'', [[:en:Tokugawa Ienari|Tokugawa Ienari]]. In 1847, at age 1, he was adopted as the heir of the 12th-generation ''daimyō'' [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]], and succeeded him in 1850, taking the name Tokugawa Yoshitomi following his coming of age in 1851. In 1858 he had audience with ''shōgun'' Iesada and his wife, Atsuhime short after he was adopted as their son and named as the successor to the main Tokugawa house. The choice of Yoshitomi was not without conflict; there were other factions in the government who supported [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] or [[:en:Matsudaira Naritami|Matsudaira Naritami]] for ''shōgun''; both of them, unlike Yoshitomi, were adults. After assuming the office of shogun, Yoshitomi changed his name to Iemochi. Before Iesada died he give his will to [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]], that: * First, [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] must help Iemochi at administration until Iemochi was old enough to rule. * Second, all political issues must be discussed with [[:en:Tenshoin|Tenshoin]], as Iemochi's mother. As part of the ''[[:en:Kōbu gattai|Kōbu gattai]]'' ("Union of Court and Bakufu") movement, On 11 February 1862, Iemochi married [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]], daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ninko|Emperor Ninko]]. Princess Kazu refused to use the title "[[:en:Midaidokoro|Midaidokoro]]", and instead only used the title "Miya". On 22 April 1863 (''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]] 3, 5th day of the 3rd month''), ''shōgun'' Iemochi traveled in a great procession to the capital. He had been summoned by the emperor, and had 3,000 retainers as escort. This was the first time since the visit of [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]] in the ''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]]'' era, 230 years before, that a shogun had visited Kyoto.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: the Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 325. His early death at the age of 20 put an end to his short marriage with princess Kazu-no-Miya. Before he died he adopted a son, Tayasu Kamenosuke (later known as [[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]]), as his heir. At that time Tayasu Kamenosuke was only 3 years old, but as the Tokugawa shogunate was [[:en:Second Chōshū expedition|at war with Chōshū]], the adult [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] was appointed the fifteenth ''shōgun''. ''Shōgun'' Yoshinobu then adopted Iemochi's adopted son, Tayasu Kamenosuke. After Iemochi's death, Kazu-no-Miya changed her name to Seikan'in no Miya. The cause of Iemochi's death is widely reported as heart failure due to [[:en:Beriberi|Beriberi]], a disease caused by [[:en:thiamine|thiamine]] (vitamin B1) deficiency. His successor, Tokugawa Yoshinobu, was the last Tokugawa ''shōgun'' and witnessed the end of the shogunate, which gave way to the [[Meiji Restoration]]. ==Family== * Father: [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) * Mother: [[Jitsujoin]] (1821–1904) * Adoptive fathers: ** [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] (1820–1849) ** [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] * Adoptive Mother: [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Wife: [[:en:Kazu-no-Miya Chikako|Kazu-no-Miya Chikako]] * Concubine: ** [[Oyuri no Kata]] (1850–1880) later Shoko-in ** Ohina no Kata (1846–1862) daughter of Shimazu Tadafuyu also niece of [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Adopted son: **[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] **[[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]] ==Eras of Iemochi's ''bakufu''== [[File:Abraham Lincoln to the Shogun of Japan 14 November 1861.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Abraham Lincoln|Abraham Lincoln]] to ''shōgun'' Tokugawa Iemochi announcing the departure of [[:en:Townsend Harris|Townsend Harris]]. 14 November 1861.]] [[File:Letter of Napoleon III to the Japanese Shogun to introduce Leon Roches in replacement of Duchesne de Bellecourt.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Napoleon III|Napoleon III]] to "Taïcoun" Tokugawa Iemochi nominating [[:en:Léon Roches|Léon Roches]], in replacement of [[:en:Duchesne de Bellecourt|Duchesne de Bellecourt]], 27 October 1863. [[:en:Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)]].]] The years in which Iemochi was ''shōgun'' are more specifically identified by more than one [[:en:Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[:en:Nengō|Nengō]]''. * ''[[:en:Ansei|Ansei]]'' (1854–1860) * ''[[:en:Man'en|Man'en]]'' (1860–1861) * ''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]]'' (1861–1864) * ''[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]]'' (1864–1865) * ''[[:en:Keiō|Keiō]]'' (1865–1868) ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== *''Rekishi Dokuhon'' Jan. 2006 issue: ''Tokugawa Shōgun-ke to Matsudaira Ichizoku'' *''Tokugawa Iemochi to sono jidai: wakaki shōgun no shōgai'' 徳川家茂とその時代: 若き将軍の生涯. Tokyo: Tokugawa kin'en zaidan 徳川記念財団, 2007. * Totman, Conrad. (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tMFPbDrEPtkC ''The Collapse of the Tokugawa Bakufu, 1862–1868.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawai'i Press|University of Hawai'i Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-0614-9}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline}} * Digitized woodblock print: [http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm Shogun Iemochi in procession towards Kyoto in ''Bunkyū 3''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071206043308/http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm |date=2007-12-06 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy}} {{succession box | title=[[:en:Kishū Domain|Lord of Kishū]]:

family|WD=Q11071640}}|native_name=徳川家茂|resting_place={{ill|Zōjō-ji|WD=Q249139}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q348466]] Wikidata name: Tokugawa Iemochi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|14th shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate of Japan}} {{family name hatnote|[[Tokugawa (surname)|Tokugawa]]|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Officeholder | honorific_prefix = [[Senior First Rank]] | name = Tokugawa Iemochi | native_name = {{Nobold|徳川 家茂}} | native_name_lang = ja | image = Toku14-2.jpg | order = ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' | monarch = [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Kōmei]] | term_start = 14 August 1858 | term_end = 29 August 1866 | predecessor = [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] | successor = [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | spouse = [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]] | birth_date = {{Birth date|1846|7|17|df=y}} | birth_place = [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato]], [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Japan | death_date = {{death date and age|1866|8|29|1846|7|17|df=y}} | death_place = [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]], Japan | signature = Tokugawa Iemochi kao.jpg }} {{nihongo|'''Tokugawa Iemochi'''|{{linktext|徳川}} {{linktext|家茂}}}} (17 July 1846 – 29 August 1866) was the 14th ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], who held office from 1858 to 1866. {{cite web |url=https://www.pbs.org/empires/japan/timeline_1800.html |title=Japan:Memoirs of a Secret Empire |access-date=2007-08-11 |work= PBS}} During his reign there was much internal turmoil as a result of the "re-opening" of Japan to western nations. Iemochi's reign also saw a weakening of the shogunate. Iemochi died in 1866 and was buried in [[:en:Zōjō-ji|Zōjō-ji]]. His Buddhist name was Shonmyoin. ==Biography== Iemochi, known in his childhood as Kikuchiyo (菊千代), was the eldest son of the 11th-generation [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] lord [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) with his concubine known as Jitsujoin and was born in the domain's residence in Edo (modern-day [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato-ku]] in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]). Nariyuki was a younger son of the 11th ''shōgun'', [[:en:Tokugawa Ienari|Tokugawa Ienari]]. In 1847, at age 1, he was adopted as the heir of the 12th-generation ''daimyō'' [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]], and succeeded him in 1850, taking the name Tokugawa Yoshitomi following his coming of age in 1851. In 1858 he had audience with ''shōgun'' Iesada and his wife, Atsuhime short after he was adopted as their son and named as the successor to the main Tokugawa house. The choice of Yoshitomi was not without conflict; there were other factions in the government who supported [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] or [[:en:Matsudaira Naritami|Matsudaira Naritami]] for ''shōgun''; both of them, unlike Yoshitomi, were adults. After assuming the office of shogun, Yoshitomi changed his name to Iemochi. Before Iesada died he give his will to [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]], that: * First, [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] must help Iemochi at administration until Iemochi was old enough to rule. * Second, all political issues must be discussed with [[:en:Tenshoin|Tenshoin]], as Iemochi's mother. As part of the ''[[:en:Kōbu gattai|Kōbu gattai]]'' ("Union of Court and Bakufu") movement, On 11 February 1862, Iemochi married [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]], daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ninko|Emperor Ninko]]. Princess Kazu refused to use the title "[[:en:Midaidokoro|Midaidokoro]]", and instead only used the title "Miya". On 22 April 1863 (''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]] 3, 5th day of the 3rd month''), ''shōgun'' Iemochi traveled in a great procession to the capital. He had been summoned by the emperor, and had 3,000 retainers as escort. This was the first time since the visit of [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]] in the ''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]]'' era, 230 years before, that a shogun had visited Kyoto.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: the Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 325. His early death at the age of 20 put an end to his short marriage with princess Kazu-no-Miya. Before he died he adopted a son, Tayasu Kamenosuke (later known as [[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]]), as his heir. At that time Tayasu Kamenosuke was only 3 years old, but as the Tokugawa shogunate was [[:en:Second Chōshū expedition|at war with Chōshū]], the adult [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] was appointed the fifteenth ''shōgun''. ''Shōgun'' Yoshinobu then adopted Iemochi's adopted son, Tayasu Kamenosuke. After Iemochi's death, Kazu-no-Miya changed her name to Seikan'in no Miya. The cause of Iemochi's death is widely reported as heart failure due to [[:en:Beriberi|Beriberi]], a disease caused by [[:en:thiamine|thiamine]] (vitamin B1) deficiency. His successor, Tokugawa Yoshinobu, was the last Tokugawa ''shōgun'' and witnessed the end of the shogunate, which gave way to the [[Meiji Restoration]]. ==Family== * Father: [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) * Mother: [[Jitsujoin]] (1821–1904) * Adoptive fathers: ** [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] (1820–1849) ** [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] * Adoptive Mother: [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Wife: [[:en:Kazu-no-Miya Chikako|Kazu-no-Miya Chikako]] * Concubine: ** [[Oyuri no Kata]] (1850–1880) later Shoko-in ** Ohina no Kata (1846–1862) daughter of Shimazu Tadafuyu also niece of [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Adopted son: **[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] **[[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]] ==Eras of Iemochi's ''bakufu''== [[File:Abraham Lincoln to the Shogun of Japan 14 November 1861.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Abraham Lincoln|Abraham Lincoln]] to ''shōgun'' Tokugawa Iemochi announcing the departure of [[:en:Townsend Harris|Townsend Harris]]. 14 November 1861.]] [[File:Letter of Napoleon III to the Japanese Shogun to introduce Leon Roches in replacement of Duchesne de Bellecourt.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Napoleon III|Napoleon III]] to "Taïcoun" Tokugawa Iemochi nominating [[:en:Léon Roches|Léon Roches]], in replacement of [[:en:Duchesne de Bellecourt|Duchesne de Bellecourt]], 27 October 1863. [[:en:Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)]].]] The years in which Iemochi was ''shōgun'' are more specifically identified by more than one [[:en:Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[:en:Nengō|Nengō]]''. * ''[[:en:Ansei|Ansei]]'' (1854–1860) * ''[[:en:Man'en|Man'en]]'' (1860–1861) * ''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]]'' (1861–1864) * ''[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]]'' (1864–1865) * ''[[:en:Keiō|Keiō]]'' (1865–1868) ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== *''Rekishi Dokuhon'' Jan. 2006 issue: ''Tokugawa Shōgun-ke to Matsudaira Ichizoku'' *''Tokugawa Iemochi to sono jidai: wakaki shōgun no shōgai'' 徳川家茂とその時代: 若き将軍の生涯. Tokyo: Tokugawa kin'en zaidan 徳川記念財団, 2007. * Totman, Conrad. (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tMFPbDrEPtkC ''The Collapse of the Tokugawa Bakufu, 1862–1868.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawai'i Press|University of Hawai'i Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-0614-9}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline}} * Digitized woodblock print: [http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm Shogun Iemochi in procession towards Kyoto in ''Bunkyū 3''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071206043308/http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm |date=2007-12-06 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy}} {{succession box | title=[[:en:Kishū Domain|Lord of Kishū]]:
Tokugawa Iemochi | before=[[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] | after=[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-mil}} {{succession box | title=''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'':
Tokugawa Iemochi | before=[[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] | after=[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-end}} {{Tokugawa dynasty (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa chronology (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa_officials}} {{Shoguns}} {{Authority control}} ‎]] [[en:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ar:توكوغاوا إييموتشي]] [[ca:Tokugawa Iemochi]] {{Commons category|Tokugawa Iemochi}} [[cs:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[da:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[de:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[es:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[eu:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fa:توکوگاوا ایه‌موچی]] [[fi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fo:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[hu:Tokugava Iemocsi]] [[id:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[it:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ja:徳川家茂]] [[ko:도쿠가와 이에모치]] [[no:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[pl:Iemochi Tokugawa]] [[pt:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ru:Токугава Иэмоти]] [[sk:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[sr:Токугава Ијемочи]] [[sv:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[th:โทกูงาวะ อิเอโมจิ]] [[tr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[uk:Токуґава Іємоті]] [[vi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[wuu:德川家茂]] [[zh:德川家茂]] [[zh-min-nan:Tokugawa Iemoti]] [[zh-yue:德川家茂]] {{wikidata link|Q348466}} [[Category:1846 births]] [[Category:1866 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century shōguns]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:People from Minato, Tokyo]] [[Category:People of Bakumatsu]] [[Category:Tokugawa clan]] [[Category:Tokugawa shōguns]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|14th shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate of Japan}} {{family name hatnote|[[Tokugawa (surname)|Tokugawa]]|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Officeholder | honorific_prefix = [[Senior First Rank]] | name = Tokugawa Iemochi | native_name = {{Nobold|徳川 家茂}} | native_name_lang = ja | image = Toku14-2.jpg | order = ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' | monarch = [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Kōmei]] | term_start = 14 August 1858 | term_end = 29 August 1866 | predecessor = [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] | successor = [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | spouse = [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]] | birth_date = {{Birth date|1846|7|17|df=y}} | birth_place = [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato]], [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Japan | death_date = {{death date and age|1866|8|29|1846|7|17|df=y}} | death_place = [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]], Japan | signature = Tokugawa Iemochi kao.jpg }} {{nihongo|'''Tokugawa Iemochi'''|{{linktext|徳川}} {{linktext|家茂}}}} (17 July 1846 – 29 August 1866) was the 14th ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], who held office from 1858 to 1866. {{cite web |url=https://www.pbs.org/empires/japan/timeline_1800.html |title=Japan:Memoirs of a Secret Empire |access-date=2007-08-11 |work= PBS}} During his reign there was much internal turmoil as a result of the "re-opening" of Japan to western nations. Iemochi's reign also saw a weakening of the shogunate. Iemochi died in 1866 and was buried in [[:en:Zōjō-ji|Zōjō-ji]]. His Buddhist name was Shonmyoin. ==Biography== Iemochi, known in his childhood as Kikuchiyo (菊千代), was the eldest son of the 11th-generation [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] lord [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) with his concubine known as Jitsujoin and was born in the domain's residence in Edo (modern-day [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato-ku]] in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]). Nariyuki was a younger son of the 11th ''shōgun'', [[:en:Tokugawa Ienari|Tokugawa Ienari]]. In 1847, at age 1, he was adopted as the heir of the 12th-generation ''daimyō'' [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]], and succeeded him in 1850, taking the name Tokugawa Yoshitomi following his coming of age in 1851. In 1858 he had audience with ''shōgun'' Iesada and his wife, Atsuhime short after he was adopted as their son and named as the successor to the main Tokugawa house. The choice of Yoshitomi was not without conflict; there were other factions in the government who supported [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] or [[:en:Matsudaira Naritami|Matsudaira Naritami]] for ''shōgun''; both of them, unlike Yoshitomi, were adults. After assuming the office of shogun, Yoshitomi changed his name to Iemochi. Before Iesada died he give his will to [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]], that: * First, [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] must help Iemochi at administration until Iemochi was old enough to rule. * Second, all political issues must be discussed with [[:en:Tenshoin|Tenshoin]], as Iemochi's mother. As part of the ''[[:en:Kōbu gattai|Kōbu gattai]]'' ("Union of Court and Bakufu") movement, On 11 February 1862, Iemochi married [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]], daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ninko|Emperor Ninko]]. Princess Kazu refused to use the title "[[:en:Midaidokoro|Midaidokoro]]", and instead only used the title "Miya". On 22 April 1863 (''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]] 3, 5th day of the 3rd month''), ''shōgun'' Iemochi traveled in a great procession to the capital. He had been summoned by the emperor, and had 3,000 retainers as escort. This was the first time since the visit of [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]] in the ''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]]'' era, 230 years before, that a shogun had visited Kyoto.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: the Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 325. His early death at the age of 20 put an end to his short marriage with princess Kazu-no-Miya. Before he died he adopted a son, Tayasu Kamenosuke (later known as [[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]]), as his heir. At that time Tayasu Kamenosuke was only 3 years old, but as the Tokugawa shogunate was [[:en:Second Chōshū expedition|at war with Chōshū]], the adult [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] was appointed the fifteenth ''shōgun''. ''Shōgun'' Yoshinobu then adopted Iemochi's adopted son, Tayasu Kamenosuke. After Iemochi's death, Kazu-no-Miya changed her name to Seikan'in no Miya. The cause of Iemochi's death is widely reported as heart failure due to [[:en:Beriberi|Beriberi]], a disease caused by [[:en:thiamine|thiamine]] (vitamin B1) deficiency. His successor, Tokugawa Yoshinobu, was the last Tokugawa ''shōgun'' and witnessed the end of the shogunate, which gave way to the [[Meiji Restoration]]. ==Family== * Father: [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) * Mother: [[Jitsujoin]] (1821–1904) * Adoptive fathers: ** [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] (1820–1849) ** [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] * Adoptive Mother: [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Wife: [[:en:Kazu-no-Miya Chikako|Kazu-no-Miya Chikako]] * Concubine: ** [[Oyuri no Kata]] (1850–1880) later Shoko-in ** Ohina no Kata (1846–1862) daughter of Shimazu Tadafuyu also niece of [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Adopted son: **[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] **[[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]] ==Eras of Iemochi's ''bakufu''== [[File:Abraham Lincoln to the Shogun of Japan 14 November 1861.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Abraham Lincoln|Abraham Lincoln]] to ''shōgun'' Tokugawa Iemochi announcing the departure of [[:en:Townsend Harris|Townsend Harris]]. 14 November 1861.]] [[File:Letter of Napoleon III to the Japanese Shogun to introduce Leon Roches in replacement of Duchesne de Bellecourt.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Napoleon III|Napoleon III]] to "Taïcoun" Tokugawa Iemochi nominating [[:en:Léon Roches|Léon Roches]], in replacement of [[:en:Duchesne de Bellecourt|Duchesne de Bellecourt]], 27 October 1863. [[:en:Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)]].]] The years in which Iemochi was ''shōgun'' are more specifically identified by more than one [[:en:Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[:en:Nengō|Nengō]]''. * ''[[:en:Ansei|Ansei]]'' (1854–1860) * ''[[:en:Man'en|Man'en]]'' (1860–1861) * ''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]]'' (1861–1864) * ''[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]]'' (1864–1865) * ''[[:en:Keiō|Keiō]]'' (1865–1868) ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== *''Rekishi Dokuhon'' Jan. 2006 issue: ''Tokugawa Shōgun-ke to Matsudaira Ichizoku'' *''Tokugawa Iemochi to sono jidai: wakaki shōgun no shōgai'' 徳川家茂とその時代: 若き将軍の生涯. Tokyo: Tokugawa kin'en zaidan 徳川記念財団, 2007. * Totman, Conrad. (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tMFPbDrEPtkC ''The Collapse of the Tokugawa Bakufu, 1862–1868.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawai'i Press|University of Hawai'i Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-0614-9}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline}} * Digitized woodblock print: [http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm Shogun Iemochi in procession towards Kyoto in ''Bunkyū 3''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071206043308/http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm |date=2007-12-06 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy}} {{succession box | title=[[:en:Kishū Domain|Lord of Kishū]]:
Tokugawa Iemochi | before=[[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] | after=[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-mil}} {{succession box | title=''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'':
Tokugawa Iemochi | before=[[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] | after=[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-end}} {{Tokugawa dynasty (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa chronology (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa_officials}} {{Shoguns}} {{Authority control}} ‎]] [[en:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ar:توكوغاوا إييموتشي]] [[ca:Tokugawa Iemochi]] {{Commons category|Tokugawa Iemochi}} [[cs:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[da:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[de:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[es:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[eu:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fa:توکوگاوا ایه‌موچی]] [[fi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fo:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[hu:Tokugava Iemocsi]] [[id:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[it:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ja:徳川家茂]] [[ko:도쿠가와 이에모치]] [[no:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[pl:Iemochi Tokugawa]] [[pt:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ru:Токугава Иэмоти]] [[sk:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[sr:Токугава Ијемочи]] [[sv:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[th:โทกูงาวะ อิเอโมจิ]] [[tr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[uk:Токуґава Іємоті]] [[vi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[wuu:德川家茂]] [[zh:德川家茂]] [[zh-min-nan:Tokugawa Iemoti]] [[zh-yue:德川家茂]] {{wikidata link|Q348466}} [[Category:1846 births]] [[Category:1866 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century shōguns]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:People from Minato, Tokyo]] [[Category:People of Bakumatsu]] [[Category:Tokugawa clan]] [[Category:Tokugawa shōguns]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

July 1846 – 29 August 1866) was the 14th ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], who held office from 1858 to 1866. {{cite web |url=https://www.pbs.org/empires/japan/timeline_1800.html |title=Japan:Memoirs of a Secret Empire |access-date=2007-08-11 |work= PBS}} During his reign there was much internal turmoil as a result of the "re-opening" of Japan to western nations. Iemochi's reign also saw a weakening of the shogunate. Iemochi died in 1866 and was buried in [[:en:Zōjō-ji|Zōjō-ji]]. His Buddhist name was Shonmyoin. ==Biography== Iemochi, known in his childhood as Kikuchiyo (菊千代), was the eldest son of the 11th-generation [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] lord [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) with his concubine known as Jitsujoin and was born in the domain's residence in Edo (modern-day [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato-ku]] in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]). Nariyuki was a younger son of the 11th ''shōgun'', [[:en:Tokugawa Ienari|Tokugawa Ienari]]. In 1847, at age 1, he was adopted as the heir of the 12th-generation ''daimyō'' [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]], and succeeded him in 1850, taking the name Tokugawa Yoshitomi following his coming of age in 1851. In 1858 he had audience with ''shōgun'' Iesada and his wife, Atsuhime short after he was adopted as their son and named as the successor to the main Tokugawa house. The choice of Yoshitomi was not without conflict; there were other factions in the government who supported [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] or [[:en:Matsudaira Naritami|Matsudaira Naritami]] for ''shōgun''; both of them, unlike Yoshitomi, were adults. After assuming the office of shogun, Yoshitomi changed his name to Iemochi. Before Iesada died he give his will to [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]], that: * First, [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] must help Iemochi at administration until Iemochi was old enough to rule. * Second, all political issues must be discussed with [[:en:Tenshoin|Tenshoin]], as Iemochi's mother. As part of the ''[[:en:Kōbu gattai|Kōbu gattai]]'' ("Union of Court and Bakufu") movement, On 11 February 1862, Iemochi married [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]], daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ninko|Emperor Ninko]]. Princess Kazu refused to use the title "[[:en:Midaidokoro|Midaidokoro]]", and instead only used the title "Miya". On 22 April 1863 (''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]] 3, 5th day of the 3rd month''), ''shōgun'' Iemochi traveled in a great procession to the capital. He had been summoned by the emperor, and had 3,000 retainers as escort. This was the first time since the visit of [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]] in the ''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]]'' era, 230 years before, that a shogun had visited Kyoto.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: the Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 325. His early death at the age of 20 put an end to his short marriage with princess Kazu-no-Miya. Before he died he adopted a son, Tayasu Kamenosuke (later known as [[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]]), as his heir. At that time Tayasu Kamenosuke was only 3 years old, but as the Tokugawa shogunate was [[:en:Second Chōshū expedition|at war with Chōshū]], the adult [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] was appointed the fifteenth ''shōgun''. ''Shōgun'' Yoshinobu then adopted Iemochi's adopted son, Tayasu Kamenosuke. After Iemochi's death, Kazu-no-Miya changed her name to Seikan'in no Miya. The cause of Iemochi's death is widely reported as heart failure due to [[:en:Beriberi|Beriberi]], a disease caused by [[:en:thiamine|thiamine]] (vitamin B1) deficiency. His successor, Tokugawa Yoshinobu, was the last Tokugawa ''shōgun'' and witnessed the end of the shogunate, which gave way to the [[Meiji Restoration]]. ==Family== * Father: [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) * Mother: [[Jitsujoin]] (1821–1904) * Adoptive fathers: ** [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] (1820–1849) ** [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] * Adoptive Mother: [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Wife: [[:en:Kazu-no-Miya Chikako|Kazu-no-Miya Chikako]] * Concubine: ** [[Oyuri no Kata]] (1850–1880) later Shoko-in ** Ohina no Kata (1846–1862) daughter of Shimazu Tadafuyu also niece of [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Adopted son: **[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] **[[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]] ==Eras of Iemochi's ''bakufu''== [[File:Abraham Lincoln to the Shogun of Japan 14 November 1861.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Abraham Lincoln|Abraham Lincoln]] to ''shōgun'' Tokugawa Iemochi announcing the departure of [[:en:Townsend Harris|Townsend Harris]]. 14 November 1861.]] [[File:Letter of Napoleon III to the Japanese Shogun to introduce Leon Roches in replacement of Duchesne de Bellecourt.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Napoleon III|Napoleon III]] to "Taïcoun" Tokugawa Iemochi nominating [[:en:Léon Roches|Léon Roches]], in replacement of [[:en:Duchesne de Bellecourt|Duchesne de Bellecourt]], 27 October 1863. [[:en:Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)]].]] The years in which Iemochi was ''shōgun'' are more specifically identified by more than one [[:en:Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[:en:Nengō|Nengō]]''. * ''[[:en:Ansei|Ansei]]'' (1854–1860) * ''[[:en:Man'en|Man'en]]'' (1860–1861) * ''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]]'' (1861–1864) * ''[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]]'' (1864–1865) * ''[[:en:Keiō|Keiō]]'' (1865–1868) ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== *''Rekishi Dokuhon'' Jan. 2006 issue: ''Tokugawa Shōgun-ke to Matsudaira Ichizoku'' *''Tokugawa Iemochi to sono jidai: wakaki shōgun no shōgai'' 徳川家茂とその時代: 若き将軍の生涯. Tokyo: Tokugawa kin'en zaidan 徳川記念財団, 2007. * Totman, Conrad. (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tMFPbDrEPtkC ''The Collapse of the Tokugawa Bakufu, 1862–1868.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawai'i Press|University of Hawai'i Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-0614-9}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline}} * Digitized woodblock print: [http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm Shogun Iemochi in procession towards Kyoto in ''Bunkyū 3''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071206043308/http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm |date=2007-12-06 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy}} {{succession box | title=[[:en:Kishū Domain|Lord of Kishū]]:

talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|14th shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate of Japan}} {{family name hatnote|[[Tokugawa (surname)|Tokugawa]]|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Officeholder | honorific_prefix = [[Senior First Rank]] | name = Tokugawa Iemochi | native_name = {{Nobold|徳川 家茂}} | native_name_lang = ja | image = Toku14-2.jpg | order = ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' | monarch = [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Kōmei]] | term_start = 14 August 1858 | term_end = 29 August 1866 | predecessor = [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] | successor = [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | spouse = [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]] | birth_date = {{Birth date|1846|7|17|df=y}} | birth_place = [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato]], [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Japan | death_date = {{death date and age|1866|8|29|1846|7|17|df=y}} | death_place = [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]], Japan | signature = Tokugawa Iemochi kao.jpg }} {{nihongo|'''Tokugawa Iemochi'''|{{linktext|徳川}} {{linktext|家茂}}}} (17 July 1846 – 29 August 1866) was the 14th ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], who held office from 1858 to 1866. {{cite web |url=https://www.pbs.org/empires/japan/timeline_1800.html |title=Japan:Memoirs of a Secret Empire |access-date=2007-08-11 |work= PBS}} During his reign there was much internal turmoil as a result of the "re-opening" of Japan to western nations. Iemochi's reign also saw a weakening of the shogunate. Iemochi died in 1866 and was buried in [[:en:Zōjō-ji|Zōjō-ji]]. His Buddhist name was Shonmyoin. ==Biography== Iemochi, known in his childhood as Kikuchiyo (菊千代), was the eldest son of the 11th-generation [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] lord [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) with his concubine known as Jitsujoin and was born in the domain's residence in Edo (modern-day [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato-ku]] in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]). Nariyuki was a younger son of the 11th ''shōgun'', [[:en:Tokugawa Ienari|Tokugawa Ienari]]. In 1847, at age 1, he was adopted as the heir of the 12th-generation ''daimyō'' [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]], and succeeded him in 1850, taking the name Tokugawa Yoshitomi following his coming of age in 1851. In 1858 he had audience with ''shōgun'' Iesada and his wife, Atsuhime short after he was adopted as their son and named as the successor to the main Tokugawa house. The choice of Yoshitomi was not without conflict; there were other factions in the government who supported [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] or [[:en:Matsudaira Naritami|Matsudaira Naritami]] for ''shōgun''; both of them, unlike Yoshitomi, were adults. After assuming the office of shogun, Yoshitomi changed his name to Iemochi. Before Iesada died he give his will to [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]], that: * First, [[:en:Ii Naosuke|Ii Naosuke]] must help Iemochi at administration until Iemochi was old enough to rule. * Second, all political issues must be discussed with [[:en:Tenshoin|Tenshoin]], as Iemochi's mother. As part of the ''[[:en:Kōbu gattai|Kōbu gattai]]'' ("Union of Court and Bakufu") movement, On 11 February 1862, Iemochi married [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]], daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ninko|Emperor Ninko]]. Princess Kazu refused to use the title "[[:en:Midaidokoro|Midaidokoro]]", and instead only used the title "Miya". On 22 April 1863 (''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]] 3, 5th day of the 3rd month''), ''shōgun'' Iemochi traveled in a great procession to the capital. He had been summoned by the emperor, and had 3,000 retainers as escort. This was the first time since the visit of [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]] in the ''[[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]]'' era, 230 years before, that a shogun had visited Kyoto.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: the Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 325. His early death at the age of 20 put an end to his short marriage with princess Kazu-no-Miya. Before he died he adopted a son, Tayasu Kamenosuke (later known as [[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]]), as his heir. At that time Tayasu Kamenosuke was only 3 years old, but as the Tokugawa shogunate was [[:en:Second Chōshū expedition|at war with Chōshū]], the adult [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] was appointed the fifteenth ''shōgun''. ''Shōgun'' Yoshinobu then adopted Iemochi's adopted son, Tayasu Kamenosuke. After Iemochi's death, Kazu-no-Miya changed her name to Seikan'in no Miya. The cause of Iemochi's death is widely reported as heart failure due to [[:en:Beriberi|Beriberi]], a disease caused by [[:en:thiamine|thiamine]] (vitamin B1) deficiency. His successor, Tokugawa Yoshinobu, was the last Tokugawa ''shōgun'' and witnessed the end of the shogunate, which gave way to the [[Meiji Restoration]]. ==Family== * Father: [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) * Mother: [[Jitsujoin]] (1821–1904) * Adoptive fathers: ** [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] (1820–1849) ** [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] * Adoptive Mother: [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Wife: [[:en:Kazu-no-Miya Chikako|Kazu-no-Miya Chikako]] * Concubine: ** [[Oyuri no Kata]] (1850–1880) later Shoko-in ** Ohina no Kata (1846–1862) daughter of Shimazu Tadafuyu also niece of [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]] * Adopted son: **[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] **[[:en:Tokugawa Iesato|Tokugawa Iesato]] ==Eras of Iemochi's ''bakufu''== [[File:Abraham Lincoln to the Shogun of Japan 14 November 1861.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Abraham Lincoln|Abraham Lincoln]] to ''shōgun'' Tokugawa Iemochi announcing the departure of [[:en:Townsend Harris|Townsend Harris]]. 14 November 1861.]] [[File:Letter of Napoleon III to the Japanese Shogun to introduce Leon Roches in replacement of Duchesne de Bellecourt.jpg|thumb|Letter of [[:en:Napoleon III|Napoleon III]] to "Taïcoun" Tokugawa Iemochi nominating [[:en:Léon Roches|Léon Roches]], in replacement of [[:en:Duchesne de Bellecourt|Duchesne de Bellecourt]], 27 October 1863. [[:en:Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)]].]] The years in which Iemochi was ''shōgun'' are more specifically identified by more than one [[:en:Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[:en:Nengō|Nengō]]''. * ''[[:en:Ansei|Ansei]]'' (1854–1860) * ''[[:en:Man'en|Man'en]]'' (1860–1861) * ''[[:en:Bunkyū|Bunkyū]]'' (1861–1864) * ''[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]]'' (1864–1865) * ''[[:en:Keiō|Keiō]]'' (1865–1868) ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== *''Rekishi Dokuhon'' Jan. 2006 issue: ''Tokugawa Shōgun-ke to Matsudaira Ichizoku'' *''Tokugawa Iemochi to sono jidai: wakaki shōgun no shōgai'' 徳川家茂とその時代: 若き将軍の生涯. Tokyo: Tokugawa kin'en zaidan 徳川記念財団, 2007. * Totman, Conrad. (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tMFPbDrEPtkC ''The Collapse of the Tokugawa Bakufu, 1862–1868.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawai'i Press|University of Hawai'i Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-0614-9}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline}} * Digitized woodblock print: [http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm Shogun Iemochi in procession towards Kyoto in ''Bunkyū 3''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071206043308/http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm |date=2007-12-06 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy}} {{succession box | title=[[:en:Kishū Domain|Lord of Kishū]]:
Tokugawa Iemochi | before=[[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] | after=[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-mil}} {{succession box | title=''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'':
Tokugawa Iemochi | before=[[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] | after=[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-end}} {{Tokugawa dynasty (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa chronology (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa_officials}} {{Shoguns}} {{Authority control}} ‎]] [[en:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ar:توكوغاوا إييموتشي]] [[ca:Tokugawa Iemochi]] {{Commons category|Tokugawa Iemochi}} [[cs:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[da:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[de:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[es:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[eu:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fa:توکوگاوا ایه‌موچی]] [[fi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fo:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[hu:Tokugava Iemocsi]] [[id:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[it:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ja:徳川家茂]] [[ko:도쿠가와 이에모치]] [[no:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[pl:Iemochi Tokugawa]] [[pt:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ru:Токугава Иэмоти]] [[sk:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[sr:Токугава Ијемочи]] [[sv:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[th:โทกูงาวะ อิเอโมจิ]] [[tr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[uk:Токуґава Іємоті]] [[vi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[wuu:德川家茂]] [[zh:德川家茂]] [[zh-min-nan:Tokugawa Iemoti]] [[zh-yue:德川家茂]] {{wikidata link|Q348466}} [[Category:1846 births]] [[Category:1866 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century shōguns]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:People from Minato, Tokyo]] [[Category:People of Bakumatsu]] [[Category:Tokugawa clan]] [[Category:Tokugawa shōguns]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

Officeholder | honorific_prefix = [[Senior First Rank]] | name = Tokugawa Iemochi | native_name = {{Nobold|徳川 家茂}} | native_name_lang = ja | image = Toku14-2.jpg | order = ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' | monarch = {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Kōmei}} | term_start = 14 August 1858 | term_end = 29 August 1866 | predecessor = {{ill|Tokugawa Iesada|en|Tokugawa Iesada|WD=Q336383|lt=Tokugawa Iesada}} | successor = [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | spouse = [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]] | birth_date = {{Birth date|1846|7|17|df=y}} | birth_place = {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|WD=Q190088|lt=Minato}}, {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}}, Japan | death_date = {{death date and age|1866|8|29|1846|7|17|df=y}} | death_place = {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle}}, Japan | signature = Tokugawa Iemochi kao.jpg }} {{nihongo|'''Tokugawa Iemochi'''|{{linktext|徳川}} {{linktext|家茂}}}} (17 July 1846 – 29 August 1866) was the 14th ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, who held office from 1858 to 1866. {{cite web |url=https://www.pbs.org/empires/japan/timeline_1800.html |title=Japan:Memoirs of a Secret Empire |access-date=2007-08-11 |work= PBS}} During his reign there was much internal turmoil as a result of the "re-opening" of Japan to western nations. Iemochi's reign also saw a weakening of the shogunate. Iemochi died in 1866 and was buried in {{ill|Zōjō-ji|en|Zōjō-ji|WD=Q249139|lt=Zōjō-ji}}. His Buddhist name was Shonmyoin. ==Biography== Iemochi, known in his childhood as Kikuchiyo (菊千代), was the eldest son of the 11th-generation [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] lord [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) with his concubine known as Jitsujoin and was born in the domain's residence in Edo (modern-day {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|WD=Q190088|lt=Minato-ku}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}). Nariyuki was a younger son of the 11th ''shōgun'', {{ill|Tokugawa Ienari|en|Tokugawa Ienari|WD=Q439717|lt=Tokugawa Ienari}}. In 1847, at age 1, he was adopted as the heir of the 12th-generation ''daimyō'' [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]], and succeeded him in 1850, taking the name Tokugawa Yoshitomi following his coming of age in 1851. In 1858 he had audience with ''shōgun'' Iesada and his wife, Atsuhime short after he was adopted as their son and named as the successor to the main Tokugawa house. The choice of Yoshitomi was not without conflict; there were other factions in the government who supported [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] or {{ill|Matsudaira Naritami|en|Matsudaira Naritami|WD=Q11530462|lt=Matsudaira Naritami}} for ''shōgun''; both of them, unlike Yoshitomi, were adults. After assuming the office of shogun, Yoshitomi changed his name to Iemochi. Before Iesada died he give his will to {{ill|Ii Naosuke|en|Ii Naosuke|WD=Q716940|lt=Ii Naosuke}}, that: * First, {{ill|Ii Naosuke|en|Ii Naosuke|WD=Q716940|lt=Ii Naosuke}} must help Iemochi at administration until Iemochi was old enough to rule. * Second, all political issues must be discussed with [[:en:Tenshoin|Tenshoin]], as Iemochi's mother. As part of the ''{{ill|Kōbu gattai|en|Kōbu gattai|WD=Q1189374|lt=Kōbu gattai}}'' ("Union of Court and Bakufu") movement, On 11 February 1862, Iemochi married [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]], daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ninko|Emperor Ninko]]. Princess Kazu refused to use the title "{{ill|Midaidokoro|en|Midaidokoro|WD=Q841474|lt=Midaidokoro}}", and instead only used the title "Miya". On 22 April 1863 (''{{ill|Bunkyū|en|Bunkyū|WD=Q730266|lt=Bunkyū}} 3, 5th day of the 3rd month''), ''shōgun'' Iemochi traveled in a great procession to the capital. He had been summoned by the emperor, and had 3,000 retainers as escort. This was the first time since the visit of {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Iemitsu}} in the ''{{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei}}'' era, 230 years before, that a shogun had visited Kyoto.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: the Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 325. His early death at the age of 20 put an end to his short marriage with princess Kazu-no-Miya. Before he died he adopted a son, Tayasu Kamenosuke (later known as {{ill|Tokugawa Iesato|en|Tokugawa Iesato|WD=Q672741|lt=Tokugawa Iesato}}), as his heir. At that time Tayasu Kamenosuke was only 3 years old, but as the Tokugawa shogunate was {{ill|Second Chōshū expedition|en|Second Chōshū expedition|WD=Q711793|lt=at war with Chōshū}}, the adult [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] was appointed the fifteenth ''shōgun''. ''Shōgun'' Yoshinobu then adopted Iemochi's adopted son, Tayasu Kamenosuke. After Iemochi's death, Kazu-no-Miya changed her name to Seikan'in no Miya. The cause of Iemochi's death is widely reported as heart failure due to {{ill|Beriberi|en|Beriberi|WD=Q4138763|lt=Beriberi}}, a disease caused by {{ill|thiamine|en|thiamine|WD=Q83187|lt=thiamine}} (vitamin B1) deficiency. His successor, Tokugawa Yoshinobu, was the last Tokugawa ''shōgun'' and witnessed the end of the shogunate, which gave way to the [[Meiji Restoration]]. ==Family== * Father: [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) * Mother: [[Jitsujoin]] (1821–1904) * Adoptive fathers: ** [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] (1820–1849) ** {{ill|Tokugawa Iesada|en|Tokugawa Iesada|WD=Q336383|lt=Tokugawa Iesada}} * Adoptive Mother: {{ill|Tenshō-in|en|Tenshō-in|WD=Q1063767|lt=Tenshō-in}} * Wife: [[:en:Kazu-no-Miya Chikako|Kazu-no-Miya Chikako]] * Concubine: ** [[Oyuri no Kata]] (1850–1880) later Shoko-in ** Ohina no Kata (1846–1862) daughter of Shimazu Tadafuyu also niece of {{ill|Tenshō-in|en|Tenshō-in|WD=Q1063767|lt=Tenshō-in}} * Adopted son: **[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] **{{ill|Tokugawa Iesato|en|Tokugawa Iesato|WD=Q672741|lt=Tokugawa Iesato}} ==Eras of Iemochi's ''bakufu''== [[File:Abraham Lincoln to the Shogun of Japan 14 November 1861.jpg|thumb|Letter of {{ill|Abraham Lincoln|en|Abraham Lincoln|WD=Q91|lt=Abraham Lincoln}} to ''shōgun'' Tokugawa Iemochi announcing the departure of {{ill|Townsend Harris|en|Townsend Harris|WD=Q610626|lt=Townsend Harris}}. 14 November 1861.]] [[File:Letter of Napoleon III to the Japanese Shogun to introduce Leon Roches in replacement of Duchesne de Bellecourt.jpg|thumb|Letter of {{ill|Napoleon III|en|Napoleon III|WD=Q7721|lt=Napoleon III}} to "Taïcoun" Tokugawa Iemochi nominating {{ill|Léon Roches|en|Léon Roches|WD=Q972846|lt=Léon Roches}}, in replacement of [[:en:Duchesne de Bellecourt|Duchesne de Bellecourt]], 27 October 1863. [[:en:Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)]].]] The years in which Iemochi was ''shōgun'' are more specifically identified by more than one [[:en:Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[:en:Nengō|Nengō]]''. * ''{{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|WD=Q1327259|lt=Ansei}}'' (1854–1860) * ''{{ill|Man'en|en|Man'en|WD=Q1193862|lt=Man'en}}'' (1860–1861) * ''{{ill|Bunkyū|en|Bunkyū|WD=Q730266|lt=Bunkyū}}'' (1861–1864) * ''[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]]'' (1864–1865) * ''{{ill|Keiō|en|Keiō|WD=Q776775|lt=Keiō}}'' (1865–1868) ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== *''Rekishi Dokuhon'' Jan. 2006 issue: ''Tokugawa Shōgun-ke to Matsudaira Ichizoku'' *''Tokugawa Iemochi to sono jidai: wakaki shōgun no shōgai'' 徳川家茂とその時代: 若き将軍の生涯. Tokyo: Tokugawa kin'en zaidan 徳川記念財団, 2007. * Totman, Conrad. (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tMFPbDrEPtkC ''The Collapse of the Tokugawa Bakufu, 1862–1868.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawai'i Press|University of Hawai'i Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-0614-9}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline}} * Digitized woodblock print: [http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm Shogun Iemochi in procession towards Kyoto in ''Bunkyū 3''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071206043308/http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm |date=2007-12-06 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy}} {{succession box | title={{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Lord of Kishū}}:
Tokugawa Iemochi | before=[[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] | after=[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-mil}} {{succession box | title=''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'':
Tokugawa Iemochi | before={{ill|Tokugawa Iesada|en|Tokugawa Iesada|WD=Q336383|lt=Tokugawa Iesada}} | after=[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-end}} {{Tokugawa dynasty (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa chronology (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa_officials}} {{Shoguns}} {{Authority control}} ‎]] [[en:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ar:توكوغاوا إييموتشي]] [[ca:Tokugawa Iemochi]] {{Commons category|Tokugawa Iemochi}} [[cs:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[da:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[de:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[es:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[eu:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fa:توکوگاوا ایه‌موچی]] [[fi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fo:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[hu:Tokugava Iemocsi]] [[id:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[it:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ja:徳川家茂]] [[ko:도쿠가와 이에모치]] [[no:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[pl:Iemochi Tokugawa]] [[pt:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ru:Токугава Иэмоти]] [[sk:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[sr:Токугава Ијемочи]] [[sv:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[th:โทกูงาวะ อิเอโมจิ]] [[tr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[uk:Токуґава Іємоті]] [[vi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[wuu:德川家茂]] [[zh:德川家茂]] [[zh-min-nan:Tokugawa Iemoti]] [[zh-yue:德川家茂]] {{wikidata link|Q348466}} [[Category:1846 births]] [[Category:1866 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century shōguns]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:People from Minato, Tokyo]] [[Category:People of Bakumatsu]] [[Category:Tokugawa clan]] [[Category:Tokugawa shōguns]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

As part of the ''{{ill|Kōbu gattai|en|Kōbu gattai|WD=Q1189374|lt=Kōbu gattai}}'' ("Union of Court and Bakufu") movement, On 11 February 1862, Iemochi married [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]], daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ninko|Emperor Ninko]]. Princess Kazu refused to use the title "{{ill|Midaidokoro|en|Midaidokoro|WD=Q841474|lt=Midaidokoro}}", and instead only used the title "Miya". On 22 April 1863 (''{{ill|Bunkyū|en|Bunkyū|WD=Q730266|lt=Bunkyū}} 3, 5th day of the 3rd month''), ''shōgun'' Iemochi traveled in a great procession to the capital. He had been summoned by the emperor, and had 3,000 retainers as escort. This was the first time since the visit of {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Iemitsu}} in the ''{{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei}}'' era, 230 years before, that a shogun had visited Kyoto.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: the Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 325. His early death at the age of 20 put an end to his short marriage with princess Kazu-no-Miya. Before he died he adopted a son, Tayasu Kamenosuke (later known as {{ill|Tokugawa Iesato|en|Tokugawa Iesato|WD=Q672741|lt=Tokugawa Iesato}}), as his heir. At that time Tayasu Kamenosuke was only 3 years old, but as the Tokugawa shogunate was {{ill|Second Chōshū expedition|en|Second Chōshū expedition|WD=Q711793|lt=at war with Chōshū}}, the adult [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] was appointed the fifteenth ''shōgun''. ''Shōgun'' Yoshinobu then adopted Iemochi's adopted son, Tayasu Kamenosuke. After Iemochi's death, Kazu-no-Miya changed her name to Seikan'in no Miya. The cause of Iemochi's death is widely reported as heart failure due to {{ill|Beriberi|en|Beriberi|WD=Q4138763|lt=Beriberi}}, a disease caused by {{ill|thiamine|en|thiamine|WD=Q83187|lt=thiamine}} (vitamin B1) deficiency. His successor, Tokugawa Yoshinobu, was the last Tokugawa ''shōgun'' and witnessed the end of the shogunate, which gave way to the [[Meiji Restoration]]. ==Family== * Father: [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) * Mother: [[Jitsujoin]] (1821–1904) * Adoptive fathers: ** [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] (1820–1849) ** {{ill|Tokugawa Iesada|en|Tokugawa Iesada|WD=Q336383|lt=Tokugawa Iesada}} * Adoptive Mother: {{ill|Tenshō-in|en|Tenshō-in|WD=Q1063767|lt=Tenshō-in}} * Wife: [[:en:Kazu-no-Miya Chikako|Kazu-no-Miya Chikako]] * Concubine: ** [[Oyuri no Kata]] (1850–1880) later Shoko-in ** Ohina no Kata (1846–1862) daughter of Shimazu Tadafuyu also niece of {{ill|Tenshō-in|en|Tenshō-in|WD=Q1063767|lt=Tenshō-in}} * Adopted son: **[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] **{{ill|Tokugawa Iesato|en|Tokugawa Iesato|WD=Q672741|lt=Tokugawa Iesato}} ==Eras of Iemochi's ''bakufu''== [[File:Abraham Lincoln to the Shogun of Japan 14 November 1861.jpg|thumb|Letter of {{ill|Abraham Lincoln|en|Abraham Lincoln|WD=Q91|lt=Abraham Lincoln}} to ''shōgun'' Tokugawa Iemochi announcing the departure of {{ill|Townsend Harris|en|Townsend Harris|WD=Q610626|lt=Townsend Harris}}. 14 November 1861.]] [[File:Letter of Napoleon III to the Japanese Shogun to introduce Leon Roches in replacement of Duchesne de Bellecourt.jpg|thumb|Letter of {{ill|Napoleon III|en|Napoleon III|WD=Q7721|lt=Napoleon III}} to "Taïcoun" Tokugawa Iemochi nominating {{ill|Léon Roches|en|Léon Roches|WD=Q972846|lt=Léon Roches}}, in replacement of [[:en:Duchesne de Bellecourt|Duchesne de Bellecourt]], 27 October 1863. [[:en:Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)]].]] The years in which Iemochi was ''shōgun'' are more specifically identified by more than one [[:en:Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[:en:Nengō|Nengō]]''. * ''{{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|WD=Q1327259|lt=Ansei}}'' (1854–1860) * ''{{ill|Man'en|en|Man'en|WD=Q1193862|lt=Man'en}}'' (1860–1861) * ''{{ill|Bunkyū|en|Bunkyū|WD=Q730266|lt=Bunkyū}}'' (1861–1864) * ''[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]]'' (1864–1865) * ''{{ill|Keiō|en|Keiō|WD=Q776775|lt=Keiō}}'' (1865–1868) ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== *''Rekishi Dokuhon'' Jan. 2006 issue: ''Tokugawa Shōgun-ke to Matsudaira Ichizoku'' *''Tokugawa Iemochi to sono jidai: wakaki shōgun no shōgai'' 徳川家茂とその時代: 若き将軍の生涯. Tokyo: Tokugawa kin'en zaidan 徳川記念財団, 2007. * Totman, Conrad. (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tMFPbDrEPtkC ''The Collapse of the Tokugawa Bakufu, 1862–1868.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawai'i Press|University of Hawai'i Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-0614-9}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline}} * Digitized woodblock print: [http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm Shogun Iemochi in procession towards Kyoto in ''Bunkyū 3''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071206043308/http://www.japankunst.de/japanbild/h0836_e.htm |date=2007-12-06 }} {{s-start}} {{s-roy}} {{succession box | title={{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Lord of Kishū}}:
Tokugawa Iemochi | before=[[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] | after=[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-mil}} {{succession box | title=''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'':
Tokugawa Iemochi | before={{ill|Tokugawa Iesada|en|Tokugawa Iesada|WD=Q336383|lt=Tokugawa Iesada}} | after=[[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | years=1858–1866}} {{s-end}} {{Tokugawa dynasty (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa chronology (Japan)}} {{Tokugawa_officials}} {{Shoguns}} {{Authority control}} ‎]] [[en:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ar:توكوغاوا إييموتشي]] [[ca:Tokugawa Iemochi]] {{Commons category|Tokugawa Iemochi}} [[cs:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[da:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[de:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[es:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[eu:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fa:توکوگاوا ایه‌موچی]] [[fi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fo:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[fr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[hu:Tokugava Iemocsi]] [[id:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[it:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ja:徳川家茂]] [[ko:도쿠가와 이에모치]] [[no:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[pl:Iemochi Tokugawa]] [[pt:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[ru:Токугава Иэмоти]] [[sk:Iemoči Tokugawa]] [[sr:Токугава Ијемочи]] [[sv:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[th:โทกูงาวะ อิเอโมจิ]] [[tr:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[uk:Токуґава Іємоті]] [[vi:Tokugawa Iemochi]] [[wuu:德川家茂]] [[zh:德川家茂]] [[zh-min-nan:Tokugawa Iemoti]] [[zh-yue:德川家茂]] {{wikidata link|Q348466}} [[Category:1846 births]] [[Category:1866 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century shōguns]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:People from Minato, Tokyo]] [[Category:People of Bakumatsu]] [[Category:Tokugawa clan]] [[Category:Tokugawa shōguns]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' | monarch = {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|WD=Q299179|lt=Kōmei}} | term_start = 14 August 1858 | term_end = 29 August 1866 | predecessor = {{ill|Tokugawa Iesada|en|Tokugawa Iesada|WD=Q336383|lt=Tokugawa Iesada}} | successor = [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] | spouse = [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]] | birth_date = {{Birth date|1846|7|17|df=y}} | birth_place = {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|WD=Q190088|lt=Minato}}, {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}}, Japan | death_date = {{death date and age|1866|8|29|1846|7|17|df=y}} | death_place = {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle}}, Japan | signature = Tokugawa Iemochi kao.jpg }} {{nihongo|'''Tokugawa Iemochi'''|{{linktext|徳川}} {{linktext|家茂}}}} (17 July 1846 – 29 August 1866) was the 14th ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, who held office from 1858 to 1866. {{cite web |url=https://www.pbs.org/empires/japan/timeline_1800.html |title=Japan:Memoirs of a Secret Empire |access-date=2007-08-11 |work= PBS}} During his reign there was much internal turmoil as a result of the "re-opening" of Japan to western nations. Iemochi's reign also saw a weakening of the shogunate. Iemochi died in 1866 and was buried in {{ill|Zōjō-ji|en|Zōjō-ji|WD=Q249139|lt=Zōjō-ji}}. His Buddhist name was Shonmyoin. ==Biography== Iemochi, known in his childhood as Kikuchiyo (菊千代), was the eldest son of the 11th-generation [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] lord [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) with his concubine known as Jitsujoin and was born in the domain's residence in Edo (modern-day {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|WD=Q190088|lt=Minato-ku}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}). Nariyuki was a younger son of the 11th ''shōgun'', {{ill|Tokugawa Ienari|en|Tokugawa Ienari|WD=Q439717|lt=Tokugawa Ienari}}. In 1847, at age 1, he was adopted as the heir of the 12th-generation ''daimyō'' [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]], and succeeded him in 1850, taking the name Tokugawa Yoshitomi following his coming of age in 1851. In 1858 he had audience with ''shōgun'' Iesada and his wife, Atsuhime short after he was adopted as their son and named as the successor to the main Tokugawa house. The choice of Yoshitomi was not without conflict; there were other factions in the government who supported [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]] or {{ill|Matsudaira Naritami|en|Matsudaira Naritami|WD=Q11530462|lt=Matsudaira Naritami}} for ''shōgun''; both of them, unlike Yoshitomi, were adults. After assuming the office of shogun, Yoshitomi changed his name to Iemochi. Before Iesada died he give his will to {{ill|Ii Naosuke|en|Ii Naosuke|WD=Q716940|lt=Ii Naosuke}}, that: * First, {{ill|Ii Naosuke|en|Ii Naosuke|WD=Q716940|lt=Ii Naosuke}} must help Iemochi at administration until Iemochi was old enough to rule. * Second, all political issues must be discussed with [[:en:Tenshoin|Tenshoin]], as Iemochi's mother. As part of the ''{{ill|Kōbu gattai|en|Kōbu gattai|WD=Q1189374|lt=Kōbu gattai}}'' ("Union of Court and Bakufu") movement, On 11 February 1862, Iemochi married [[:en:Princess Kazu|Princess Kazu]], daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ninko|Emperor Ninko]]. Princess Kazu refused to use the title "{{ill|Midaidokoro|en|Midaidokoro|WD=Q841474|lt=Midaidokoro}}", and instead only used the title "Miya". On 22 April 1863 (''{{ill|Bunkyū|en|Bunkyū|WD=Q730266|lt=Bunkyū}} 3, 5th day of the 3rd month''), ''shōgun'' Iemochi traveled in a great procession to the capital. He had been summoned by the emperor, and had 3,000 retainers as escort. This was the first time since the visit of {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Iemitsu}} in the ''{{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei}}'' era, 230 years before, that a shogun had visited Kyoto.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: the Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 325. His early death at the age of 20 put an end to his short marriage with princess Kazu-no-Miya. Before he died he adopted a son, Tayasu Kamenosuke (later known as {{ill|Tokugawa Iesato|en|Tokugawa Iesato|WD=Q672741|lt=Tokugawa Iesato}}), as his heir. At that time Tayasu Kamenosuke was only 3 years old, but as the Tokugawa shogunate was {{ill|Second Chōshū expedition|en|Second Chōshū expedition|WD=Q711793|lt=at war with Chōshū}}, the adult [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] was appointed the fifteenth ''shōgun''. ''Shōgun'' Yoshinobu then adopted Iemochi's adopted son, Tayasu Kamenosuke. After Iemochi's death, Kazu-no-Miya changed her name to Seikan'in no Miya. The cause of Iemochi's death is widely reported as heart failure due to {{ill|Beriberi|en|Beriberi|WD=Q4138763|lt=Beriberi}}, a disease caused by {{ill|thiamine|en|thiamine|WD=Q83187|lt=thiamine}} (vitamin B1) deficiency. His successor, Tokugawa Yoshinobu, was the last Tokugawa ''shōgun'' and witnessed the end of the shogunate, which gave way to the [[Meiji Restoration]]. ==Family== * Father: [[Tokugawa Nariyuki]] (1801–1846) * Mother: [[Jitsujoin]] (1821–1904) * Adoptive fathers: ** [[Tokugawa Narikatsu]] (1820–1849) ** {{ill|Tokugawa Iesada|en|Tokugawa Iesada|WD=Q336383|lt=Tokugawa Iesada}} * Adoptive Mother: {{ill|Tenshō-in|en|Tenshō-in|WD=Q1063767|lt=Tenshō-in}} * Wife: [[:en:Kazu-no-Miya Chikako|Kazu-no-Miya Chikako]] * Concubine: ** [[Oyuri no Kata]] (1850–1880) later Shoko-in ** Ohina no Kata (1846–1862) daughter of Shimazu Tadafuyu also niece of {{ill|Tenshō-in|en|Tenshō-in|WD=Q1063767|lt=Tenshō-in}} * Adopted son: **[[Tokugawa Mochitsugu]] **{{ill|Tokugawa Iesato|en|Tokugawa Iesato|WD=Q672741|lt=Tokugawa Iesato}} ==Eras of Iemochi's ''bakufu''== [[File:Abraham Lincoln to the Shogun of Japan 14 November 1861.jpg|thumb|Letter of {{ill|Abraham Lincoln|en|Abraham Lincoln|WD=Q91|lt=Abraham Lincoln}} to ''shōgun'' Tokugawa Iemochi announcing the departure of {{ill|Townsend Harris|en|Townsend Harris|WD=Q610626|lt=Townsend Harris}}. 14 November 1861.]] [[File:Letter of Napoleon III to the Japanese Shogun to introduce Leon Roches in replacement of Duchesne de Bellecourt.jpg|thumb|Letter of {{ill|Napoleon III|en|Napoleon III|WD=Q7721|lt=Napoleon III}} to "Taïcoun" Tokugawa Iemochi nominating {{ill|Léon Roches|en|Léon Roches|WD=Q972846|lt=Léon Roches}}, in replacement of [[:en:Duchesne de Bellecourt|Duchesne de Bellecourt]], 27 October 1863. [[:en:Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Diplomatic Record Office of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)]].]] The years in which Iemochi was ''shōgun'' are more specifically identified by more than one [[:en:Japanese era names|era name]] or ''[[:en:Nengō|Nengō]]''. * ''{{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|WD=Q1327259|lt=Ansei}}'' (1854–1860) * ''{{ill|Man'en|en|Man'en|WD=Q1193862|lt=Man'en}}'' (1860–1861) * ''{{ill|Bunkyū|en|Bunkyū|WD=Q730266|lt=Bunkyū}}'' (1861–1864) * ''[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]]'' (1864–1865) * ''{{ill|Keiō|en|Keiō|WD=Q776775|lt=Keiō}}'' (1865–1868) ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== *''Rekishi Dokuhon'' Jan. 2006 issue: ''Tokugawa Shōgun-ke to Matsudaira Ichizoku'' *''Tokugawa Iemochi to sono jidai: wakaki shōgun no shōgai'' 徳川家茂とその時代: 若き将軍の生涯. Tokyo: Tokugawa kin'en zaidan 徳川記念財団, 2007. * Totman, Conrad. (1980).
